data.title: Bed Bath & Beyond: This Progress Isn't Enough (Downgrade)
data.description:
Well over a year ago now, in May 2024, I decided to take a look at Bed Bath & Beyond (BBBY), which used to be known as Overstock. This business was intriguing to me, especially after seeing the share price fall so much. However, I had my reservations. While there had been a slight increase in revenue during that time, profits and cash flows had been on the decline. Management was at the time working on relaunching its platforms. But there was no guarantee that this would pave the way to a brighter future for shareholders. I acknowledged that if things went well, there could be some nice upside. But I also warned that there was meaningful risk investing in the business. I ended up deciding to rate the company a "Hold" at that time. But in retrospect, I think I was being too generous and optimistic.

Since then, shares have plummeted 58.2% at a time when the S&P 500 is up 30%. And, unfortunately, the financial picture for the business has only worsened. The good news is that the enterprise has a pretty solid balance sheet. However, the bad news is I really don't see any positive change here. With active customers plummeting and the number of orders delivered by the company declining as well, the future for the business looks bleak. Because of this, I think that downgrading the company to a "Sell" is the right choice.

Checking out Bed Bath & Beyond

The management team at Bed Bath & Beyond describes the company as an e-commerce marketing firm that is dedicated to providing products and services to customers. It owns multiple brands, including Bed Bath & Beyond, Overstock, and Zulily. Once seen as high-flying brands, these have since fallen by the wayside. And, unfortunately, management has not been successful in rekindling the fire that once fueled them.

I don't think a deep dive into results from prior fiscal years is needed. But in the chart above, you can see financial results for the 2023 through 2024 fiscal years. Revenue plummeted from $1.56 billion to just under $1.40 billion. Although the firm's net loss improved, cash flows got markedly worse. This was driven by a decline in the number of active customers utilizing its platforms. That number fell from 5.61 million in 2023 to 5.42 million last year. But that's not all. As you will see shortly, there were other contributors to this pain.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the first nine months of 2025 compared to the same time last year. You can probably tell from this that things are getting significantly worse from a revenue standpoint. Sales plummeted 29.4%, from $1.09 billion to $771.2 million. This came as the number of active customers dropped 30.4%, from 5.96 million to 4.15 million. This resulted in a 20.1% drop in the number of orders delivered by the company. The chart below shows these figures, as well as the figures for the 2023 to 2024 fiscal years.

There were some other interesting metrics to consider. On the positive side of things, the trailing 12-month net revenue per customer for the company grew to $259 compared to the $248 it was a year earlier, with that data shown in the chart below. And the average order value increased to $205 compared to the $199 it was previously. All of this does look positive. However, even within these metrics, there is some concerning data. That same chart also shows that the orders per active customer contracted from 1.39 to 1.30. This is part of a larger trend. From 2023 to 2024, there was a contraction from 1.41 to 1.37. So although revenue per customer is growing, active customers are dropping, and the number of times those who are remaining with the company order from it is declining as well. None of this is a recipe for success.

This does not mean there's no really good data. If I had to say the best thing about the most recent results that management has provided, it would be the fact that bottom-line results are showing signs of improvement. Overall net losses improved from $177.5 million to $63.7 million. Some of this improvement came from an increase in the company's gross profit margin from 20.2% to 24.7%. Management attributed this to the fact that the discontinued operations in Canada reported some operational improvements, as well as to lower return costs and lower product costs. There have been other improvements as well. As an example, sales and marketing costs dropped from 17% of revenue to 13.7%. This was caused by decreased performance marketing expenses and a reduction in brand advertising. But honestly, this is worrisome to me. Advertising is precisely how customers come to know about the business. And if it's not actively advertising, it risks continued active customer declines.

Other profitability metrics for the company improved also. Operating cash flow went from negative $152.6 million to negative $50.2 million. If we adjust for changes in working capital, we would get an improvement from negative $111.7 million to negative $28.9 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business improved from negative $116.1 million to negative $26.2 million. All of these represent reversals compared to what we saw from 2023 to 2024. So I am definitely happy about that.

One thing that I will say is that there is a good chance that margins will continue to improve in the near term. Management is making significant strides in bringing their marketing expense ratio down to 12%. That's the goal for 2026. The company has also been making certain strategic investments aimed at improving operations, including investments in AI. This included investments in AI-driven automation to cut down on labor costs. And it has also included investments in AI aimed at improving the customer experience. Management has been making other moves as well.

It made another $3 million investment, for instance, into GrainChain, which happens to be its blockchain-based supply chain platform. For those not familiar, GrainChain is a financial technology company dedicated to transforming the agricultural supply chain. In essence, its platform tracks commodities from farm through storage and transport to the buyer. They do this using sensors, data capture technologies, and smart contracts. They also helped to facilitate faster payments to farmers once delivery is verified using data. And that's because the smart contracts in question trigger automatic settlement. This also helps to reduce certain risks for lenders, storage operators, and buyers. As part of its relationship with GrainChain, Bed Bath & Beyond is getting this startup to analyze its entire supply chain to find potential operational improvements.

The company also purchased, in September of this year, Kirkland’s Home trade name and related brand assets. This $10 million purchase gives the company additional intellectual property that it can grow into, including giving it the opportunity to expand into the wholesale channel for that brand. Plus, the business has raised some capital on the market, around $113 million, for the purpose of bolstering its balance sheet. This should make it easier for the enterprise, given its net cash position of $176.4 million, to expand into non-retail, home centric technology, certain PropTech solutions, and more. The potential for another one of its investments, tZERO, which happens to be a blockchain company involved in providing tokenized offerings such as Bed Bath & Beyond’s BABY digital token, also might have some potential.

Even with all of these positive changes, management is going to have to make some major progress in order for the company to be even fairly valued. In the chart above, you can see how much adjusted operating cash flow it would need to be fairly valued at a price-to-adjusted-operating-cash-flow multiple of either 5, 10, or 15. That chart shows the same thing for EBITDA for the EV to EBITDA multiple. That's quite a hefty lift compared to where the company is at the moment.

Takeaway

To be honest with you, I should have been bearish on Bed Bath & Beyond before this. But these things happen. The important thing is that, looking at the picture today, I see a company that remains in significant trouble. The good news is that certain investments management is making and certain cost-cutting efforts are helping its bottom line. It also has a pretty strong net cash position. But as things stand, that's not enough for me to be anything other than mildly bearish. For that reason, I don't mind downgrading it to a soft "Sell" at this time.

data.title: When Will The Stock Market Bubble Burst?
data.description:
Herb Stein famously said that when something cannot go on forever, it will stop. This would seem to be true of today’s ever-inflating stock market bubble. The real question is not whether this bubble will burst. Rather, it is how and when that will happen.

According to today’s conventional wisdom, the bubble will only burst when the Federal Reserve starts to tighten monetary policy. However, there are other ways in which the bubble could burst that could long precede any Fed monetary policy tightening.

Anyone doubting that we have an artificial intelligence-induced stock bubble has not been paying attention to the many warning signals that we are in bubble territory. The S&P 500’s Cyclically Adjusted Price-Earnings Ratio is around 40.

That is more than double its long-run average and close to its 2000 dot-com bubble high. Meanwhile, the value of the stock market in relation to GDP, a measure favored by Warren Buffett, has now reached 230 percent. That is more than 50 percent higher than its previous all-time high in 2000.

Other indications of irrational exuberance are companies like OpenAI being valued at $500 billion despite the fact that it will not yield a profit for at least another few years.

It would also not seem to be healthy that the so-called Magnificent Seven companies, which are the major players in the AI space, now constitute as much as 37 percent of the overall value of the S&P 500. Another warning flag would seem to be the literally hundreds of billions of dollars being invested in AI that have accounted for close to half of US GDP growth in recent quarters.

According to the conventional wisdom, as expressed most recently by Bridgewater founder Ray Dalio, stock market bubbles only burst when the Fed starts to tighten monetary policy. If that is indeed the case, the current bubble could go on for a long time.

President Trump is presently calling on the Fed to cut interest rates by as much as three percentage points. More relevant, Trump seems to be well on his way to getting a majority of his appointees on the Fed’s Board, and he is expected to appoint a monetary policy dove to replace Jerome Powell when his term as Fed Chair expires in May. All of this makes it highly unlikely that the Fed will be raising interest rates anytime soon.

A key point that the conventional wisdom overlooks is that it is the 10-year Treasury bond yield rather than the Fed’s short-term interest rate that is the most relevant both for the economy’s performance and for discounting a company’s earnings stream for valuation purposes.

This means that if aggressive cuts in the Fed’s interest rate cause the long-term bond market yields to spike, that could be a trigger for the bursting of the stock market bubble.

In today’s context of the parlous state of the US public finances, an overly accommodative Fed could cause a spike in long-term interest rates. According to the IMF, the US budget deficit is expected to hover at around seven percent of GDP for as far as the eye can see.

That will cause the US public debt to rise to around 128 percent of GDP by 2030 or to a level greater than that in Greece and Italy. There is a real risk that, in those circumstances, a Fed monetary policy easing will be interpreted by the market as a sign that the US was trying to inflate its way out of its debt problem.

In turn, that would invite the return of the bond vigilantes and send long-term government bond yields sharply higher in a manner that would not be good for the economy or for equity valuations.

Those arguing that only a Fed tightening could cause the stock market bubble to burst are overlooking a couple of recent occurrences when the market swooned despite no change in the Fed’s monetary policy stance.

The first was the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic that caused a 34 percent decline in the S&P 500. The second was when the S&P 500 swooned by 12 percent in the week following Trump’s April 2, 2025, “liberation day” import tariff announcement.

All of this is not to say anyone knows when the stock market bubble will burst. However, it is to say that it is not a good idea to bet the farm on the notion that the stock market bubble will only burst when the Fed starts to tighten monetary policy.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Nvidia: Seismic Quarter Redefining AI Markets
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) reported spectacular growth today, with revenue hitting $57 billion in FQ3 2025, up 62.5% YoY and up 22% from the prior quarter. The growth is almost entirely driven by the Data Center segment, as Blackwell sales continue to ramp, reflecting a GPU renewal cycle after the product's full commercial launch earlier this year. Gaming, the company's second largest segment and one of the most volatile (driven by new game launches and cyclical demand), was down 1%, while automotive was up 1%. Professional Visualization revenue stood at $760 million, up 56% YoY.

The results support the consensus bullish trade - the AI infrastructure buildout is running at full speed, showing no signs of a slowdown. On a company level, it assuages concerns over competition after multiple deals with Nvidia's competitors, including the OpenAI (OPENAI)/Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) GPU deal, and hyperscalers' in-house microchips being marketed to customers, as manifested in Amazon's (AMZN) deal with Saudi Arabia's Humain, announced today, which will utilize Amazon's Trainium microchips, along with Nvidia's GPUs.

The only key weakness in the quarter was the 120 basis points YoY decline in gross margins, but it is unclear which segment contributed to the modest drop.

Short Term Outlook

The short-term fundamentals for Nvidia remain solid. Demand from hyperscalers is increasing, driven by generative AI labs and Large Language Model ("LLM") providers.

Although these entities (generative AI labs & LLM providers) are mostly unprofitable, ample funding, as manifested in recent deals – including Microsoft's (MSFT) $15 billion investment in Anthropic and OpenAI's $8.3 billion funding round that set its valuation at record highs. Funds from all over the world are pouring in, including sovereign wealth funds, as manifested in the Kuwait Wealth Fund – Brookfield (BAM) $100 billion AI infrastructure fund.

The point is, these companies, which are fueling demand for Nvidia's chips (via hyperscalers and neoclouds – e.g., CoreWeave (CRWV) and Nebius (NBIS)), are flush with cash. The AI race is real, and all players are training their models for deeper intelligence. With ample cash and fierce competition, I see steady demand for Nvidia's microchips in the coming several quarters.

The company guided for $65 billion in sales for FQ4 2025, suggesting continued momentum. That's above Wall Street's expectations of roughly $62 billion and translates to 65.4% YoY growth.

Medium Term Outlook

Circular financing, manifested in chipmakers/hyperscalers injecting cash into their customers in the form of equity and debt investments, will remain on investors' minds until LLM companies can stand on their own feet by becoming profitable and self-financing.

My view is that circular financing isn't inherently bad. Financing risk is the same, regardless of where it comes from, in terms of credit risk (holding everything else constant). If borrowers and venture firms become profitable and repay lenders and investors, circular financing is just like any other form of financing. The issue becomes a problem when there is no value-added, which is not the case here. This reframing of the issue sheds light on what is important: profits and the existence of a third party, outside these circular arrangements, that is benefiting from generative AI.

The efficiency benefits of AI are undisputed. The question is, are the benefits equal to or exceeding the investments being poured into AI? Nvidia, as the dominant supplier of GPUs, is a proxy of the market. With $65 billion in FQ4 outlook and $57 billion in FQ3 sales announced today, the company is on track to deliver ~$212 billion this fiscal year. Statista puts the generative AI market at $59 billion for 2025 (growing 37.5% through 2031, to reach $400 billion). There is other CapEx, including energy investments, but, overall, these figures suggest decent unit economics.

If AI increases the productivity of the U.S. alone by a mere 2%, that's $600 billion in value added (calculated as $30 trillion GDP estimate * 2%). Think of Schools, Hospitals, and government services all adopting generative AI, increasing efficiency and improving services. We are still in the early stages of the generative AI cycle.

Automotive is also a very bright prospect for Nvidia. Automotive currently accounts for a small portion of Nvidia's consolidated earnings but could become a strong tailwind in the medium term as autonomous driving becomes more ubiquitous. Currently, multiple automotive companies are testing Nvidia's automotive offerings in their autonomous vehicle roadmaps. Waymo, one of the leading autonomous driving operators, uses Nvidia's GPUs both inside and outside the vehicle in data centers dedicated to training autonomous driving models.

Valuation

In terms of valuation, Nvidia currently trades at a roughly 40x P/E ratio, significantly above the Information Technology sector's median. However, while this adds risks to the investment thesis, this is offset by the company's leading position and commanding product offerings. Nvidia's GPUs remain the standard for generative AI training and inference, despite competitors' efforts to gain a slice of the expansive market.

Earlier this year, Nvidia's CEO noted that pre-orders for Blackwell and Rubin have reached $500 billion, equivalent to multiple years of supply. Growth is a key factor in valuation. Assuming P/E multiples remain constant this year, we're looking at a $272/share price target (6.8 EPS estimate for FY 2027 times 40x). This translates to a 46% upside potential.

When compared to leading semiconductor firms, the company's P/E ratio doesn't seem extraordinarily high on a one-year forward basis, highlighting the importance of growth in this thesis.

Final Thoughts and Risks

With risk comes opportunity. Nvidia is growing fast, but its customers (beyond public cloud providers) are still unprofitable. With valuations above historical averages, combined with the cyclical nature of the business, investors should be prepared to hold for the long run through volatile markets.

For now, financing is ample, allowing LLM and AI Labs to embark on capital-intensive efforts to make generative AI smarter and more useful. If successful, we could see more ubiquitous adoption, fueling demand for Nvidia's products.

Competition is an important factor, but, despite attempts by Nvidia's peers, today's results highlight its leading position and competitive moat.

data.title: Rates Spark: Blinkers On Into Payrolls
data.description:
By Padhraic Garvey, CFA, Regional Head of Research, Americas and Benjamin Schroeder, Senior Rates Strategist

Some bearish impulses for Treasuries. It seems that Treasuries don't like the data fog, and with the payrolls delayed until after the December FOMC, there's a window of bearishness ahead.

Two drivers for US Treasuries - both a tad bearish

First, we head into the (delayed) September payrolls report on Thursday with the market in a very tolerant mood for a weak number. Normally, if there was a market expectation for a number of about 50k, it would be a huge miss relative to the clearing number of 150k. But that clearing number is acknowledged to be lower now, partly on account of supply-side shocks being applied to the labour market.

Also, with a new Bureau of Labor Statistics head in place (post the firing of the former one), there is likely a tendency to come out with a conservative estimate for employment numbers to avoid the downward revisions that have plagued previous versions.

This Treasury market would take a 50k jobs growth number all day long as indicative of an economy that's remaining reasonably resilient (even if deemed vulnerable). At the margin, this is a bearish impulse for Treasuries.

Second, the release of the payroll numbers for November has been delayed until after the December FOMC meeting (incorporating October). This gives the Federal Reserve an out to skip a rate cut due to the "fog" as described by Chair Powell.

The FOMC minutes from the previous meeting note that many members are not sold on the December rate cut, but that's no surprise, as Chair Powell explained at the press conference from that meeting.

Overall, the Treasury market has chosen to trade quite heavily through the "driving through the fog" period, typically being more responsive to firm data than to weak data. So, at the margin, this is also bearish for Treasuries.

Thursday's events and market views

The US will release the delayed jobs data for September, where the consensus is looking for a payrolls' growth of 50k and an unemployment rate of 4.3%. The other releases for the day will be the October existing home sales as well as the Philadelphia and Kansas Fed’s November manufacturing indices.

Fed speakers for the day will be Hammack, Barr, Cook, Goolsbee, Miran and Paulson. Markets will scrutinise their comments for any hints on the timing of the Fed’s potential next cut given the revised data release calendar from the Bureau of Labor Statistics.

In terms of issuance, we have Spain with 7Y, 10Y and 30Y SPGB auctions for a total of €5.5bn. France will auction 3Y, 5Y, 6Y and 8Y OATs amounting to €12bn, and in addition 11Y, 15Y and 28Y OATei for €1bn. From the UK we have a tender for a 27Y Gilt linker (£0.3bn) and from the US a 10Y TIPS for $19bn.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Nvidia: Huge Growth, Breath Of Relief
data.description:
Article Thesis

Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) reported its most recent earnings results on Wednesday, showcasing strong growth and beating estimates. The market reacted positively to Nvidia's earnings – not only when it comes to NVDA's stock price, but the entire NASDAQ index (QQQ) moved up as investors got some positive news for AI stocks.

Past Coverage

I have written about Nvidia Corporation here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently in September, two months ago, when I published this article covering the deal between Nvidia and Intel Corporation (INTC). I was neutral on Nvidia back then, giving the company a Hold rating – Nvidia's shares rose for some time but have pulled back in recent weeks on the back of a broad market pullback and some worries about AI stock valuations. With two months having passed since my last article on Nvidia, and with Nvidia releasing its most recent earnings results on Wednesday, it's time for me to update my thesis on this chip giant.

What Happened?

On Wednesday, following the market's close, we got one of the most important earnings releases ever – Nvidia's Q3 earnings report – at a time when many investors are uncertain about AI stock valuations and the tech industry in general. The widely anticipated earnings news was, luckily, good – Nvidia's headline results for the quarter looked like this:

We see that Nvidia, unsurprisingly, showed strong growth versus one quarter earlier. Relative to what was expected by the analyst community, the company delivered a double beat. Both the revenue consensus estimate and the consensus revenue estimate were outperformed by a couple of percentage points. While that wasn't an extraordinarily large beat, it was a beat nevertheless – and the market breathed a sigh of relief as AI champion Nvidia performed better than what was anticipated, with NVDA trading up by 3% at the time of writing while the QQQ ETF is up by around 1% following Nvidia's earnings report. Let's delve into the numbers.

Nvidia's Q3 Performance

Absolutely no one was surprised by the fact that Nvidia delivered strong business growth versus one year earlier, although analysts had forecasted a slightly smaller growth rate. At 63%, relative to one year earlier, Nvidia grew its revenues at a very strong pace in absolute terms, and Nvidia also managed to show a better year-over-year revenue growth rate compared to one quarter earlier. That was a nice feat, as Nvidia's year-over-year growth rate had declined for several quarters in a row before that. But in Q3, with year-over-year growth coming in at 63%, Nvidia grew meaningfully faster than in Q2, when it reported a growth rate of 56%, although growth remained below what we saw in Q1 and the seven quarters before that.

Of course, as a company grows in size, maintaining an extraordinarily high growth rate becomes harder and harder, which is why it's no surprise that Nvidia isn't growing at the 100%+ growth rates we saw a year ago. For a company as large as Nvidia, a 60%+ annual growth rate is still excellent, I believe.

Nvidia's revenue growth largely stemmed from its data center business, where Nvidia benefits like no other company from the huge investments by Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL), Meta Platforms (META), Amazon.com (AMZN), and many more. It feels like every week, we get a new announcement about a major data center from these or other tech companies, and Nvidia is providing the chips for most of these data centers – Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) is only a minor competitor, at least for now. Nvidia's data center business contributed around 90% of Nvidia's overall revenues, and the data center business showed revenue growth of 66% versus one year earlier. As long as this unit continues to perform excellently, this isn't a problem, but it's still noteworthy that Nvidia's other business units, such as Gaming, Automotive, Robotics, etc., delivered a way slower growth rate. Nvidia's growth as a company is thus highly dependent on its data center business – once we see a slowdown here, Nvidia's company-wide growth rate will decline meaningfully.

On the profit side, things looked good overall, although not everything was great. Nvidia's gross margin declined versus one year earlier, with the company's GAAP gross margin dropping by 120 base points versus one year earlier, while Nvidia's non-GAAP gross margin pulled back by 140 base points over the same time frame. Nvidia's gross margins are still pretty high, at 73% to 74%, but not quite as high as they were a year ago. Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (TSM) knows about its market power and is willing to demand a larger portion of the pie via price increases – and TSM continued to increase prices in November. It wouldn't at all be disastrous if Nvidia's gross margins were to fall to 70% or so eventually, either due to TSM's price increases or due to potentially growing competition from AMD. But while that wouldn't be a huge problem, it would put a bit of pressure on Nvidia's profit trajectory.

The good news is that Nvidia has been able to offset gross margin headwinds via operating leverage in Q3: Operating expenses, for research and development, marketing, administration, and so on rose by close to 40% in Q3 – a big increase, but a much smaller increase than Nvidia's revenue growth. Operating leverage allowed Nvidia to grow its operating income by 62% on a non-GAAP basis, almost perfectly in line with the revenue growth rate; thus, the gross margin decline was fully balanced out by below-average operating cost growth.

Net income and earnings per share grew a little less than operating profits, but this was largely due to taxes rising more than operating income. This can revert in the future, as taxes can move up and down on a quarterly basis due to factors such as timing, M&A, and so on; thus, I don't see a reason to worry about net income and earnings per share growing a little less than operating income.

Nvidia's Growth Outlook

With its upcoming Instinct MI450, AMD could become a more viable competitor in the future, but it is, I believe, highly unlikely that Nvidia would lose a lot of market share even if AMD is successful. The market for AI chips also keeps growing quickly, and many hyperscalers have plans to ramp up their spending over the coming years. There is thus ample growth potential for Nvidia in the foreseeable future, although it's pretty clear that maintaining the current growth rate forever is impossible – no company can grow at 60% or anywhere close to it forever, and Nvidia's customers naturally can't grow their capital expenditures at 60% per year forever.

For the current quarter, Nvidia's management guides for revenues of around $65 billion, implying the company's revenue growth rate will remain at a very attractive level in the 60% to 70% range this quarter. Next year, analysts forecast a revenue increase of 40%, with a slowdown to around 25% being expected for the year after that. While that is still a very attractive growth rate, one can argue that it's accounted for in Nvidia's valuation already. The company trades at a little more than 40x this year's expected net profits, which isn't a low valuation at all. With a market capitalization of around $4.5 trillion, Nvidia also looks pricey relative to its free cash flows, which totaled $58 billion so far this year – that's less than $80 billion annualized.

I'm not bearish on Nvidia at all, but I'm also not very bullish right here. Nvidia keeps executing well and should benefit from market growth going forward, but growth will inevitably slow down in the coming years, and relative to the earnings the company generates, and especially relative to the free cash flows Nvidia generates, the stock is far from cheap.

I'm happy for longs and think it's very good for the market that Nvidia beat estimates at a time when many tech investors were jittery, but I'm not deploying money into Nvidia at the current valuation.

data.title: FTAI Aviation: Short-Term Consolidation Creates Buying Opportunity
data.description:
FTAI's Q3 Earnings Call

Since I initiated coverage in August with a Buy rating, FTAI has continued to fire on all cylinders. First, FTAI released a strong earnings report, in which management raised guidance for 2026 Adjusted EBITDA from $1.4 billion to $1.525 billion, and raised the dividend by 16%. Importantly, free cash flow in 2026 is guided for $1 billion, a 33% increase.

During the earnings call, FTAI also announced that the strategic capital initiative, or SCI, finally closed at $6 billion, double their initial funding goal of $3 billion:

With the $500 million increase in equity capital, our new target is now to deploy over $6 billion in capital through the 2025 partnership, up from our previous target of $4 billion and double the original goal of $3 billion we announced in December of last year when we launched SCI. This expanded partnership corresponds to a larger total portfolio size of approximately 375 aircraft with full deployment of capital now anticipated by mid-2026.

As I explained in my prior article, the SCI is a fund that FTAI manages that invests in airplane leasing. It represents a new asset-light business model that allows the company to have access to more planes to repair, without having to put up the money to buy them as they did in their traditional leasing business.

The earnings call was well-received by analysts, with buy ratings across the board. Jefferies noted that if the model remains on track, FTAI shares could reach $500 by 2028. Fellow Seeking Alpha contributor Ricardo Fernandez broke down this report in greater detail, issuing a buy rating and a price target of $236.

Other Positive Developments

Before the call, FTAI partnered with Finair to provide perpetual engine exchanges. Shortly afterwards, FTAI received approval for their HPT stage 1 blade module, which analysts say will generate $300k per engine module.

More recently, FTAI announced a partnership with Palantir (PLTR) to use AI to optimize their workflow.

Short Term Consolidation

Earlier this year, FTAI plunged due to a short report. This report was later found to be meritless, causing FTAI to rally roughly 100% from the lows back to all time highs. Now, FTAI seems to be taking a bit of a breather, consolidating around the $150-160 levels, despite the plethora of good news. This reminds me a bit of Astera Labs (ALAB), which had such a period of consolidation following good news earlier this year. It then had a massive rally, although it appears to be experiencing another such dip now. The main difference is that Astera Labs currently has a P/E ratio of 126, while FTAI trades at a more modest 37 - and a forward P/E of just 23. In other words, FTAI isn't just a growth stock to buy on a dip, it's actually close to value territory at these multiples.

Management Buys Shares

On November 13 and 14, the COO, CEO, and CFO made 4 insider purchases of FTAI shares. These buys together were $1.4 million, which represents an even larger dollar amount than when the short report was written earlier this year. In fact, these two days in November represent more insider buying than the rest of the year combined. Management is putting their money where their mouth is.

Upcoming Catalyst: Lower Interest Rates

In 2026, Trump will name a new Fed Chair, and Trump has said he wants one who will aggressively lower interest rates. Lower interest rates will help FTAI in a variety of ways. First, it could allow for the company to refinance their relatively high debt load at lower rates. Second, lower rates tend to support higher valuation multiples, especially for growth companies like FTAI. Third, lower rates increase demand for alternative asset classes that provide reliable yield, such as FTAI's SCI fund.

Risks

In August, I said the main risk to FTAI was a global economic slowdown, which would hurt the aviation industry as well as potentially blunt appetite for the SCI. Unfortunately, fears about the global economy do not appear to be abating, so this risk factor can't be discounted.

The Bottom Line

FTAI continues to execute, proving the efficacy of their new business model. Shares, however, are still consolidating after the last leg up, and price multiples are fairly modest.

I have increased my position and averaged up, and I maintain the price targets I set in August, seeing fair value for FTAI at $200 by the end of 2025 and $300 by the end of 2026. The $500 by 2028 target from Jefferies is reasonable as well.

data.title: Nvidia: Keep Buying After Strong Q3 Results
data.description:
After the bell on Wednesday, we received fiscal third quarter results from chip giant Nvidia Corporation (NVDA) for its October-ending period. The company has been one of the best growth stories in the market in recent years, and investors have high expectations for continued strong growth moving forward. Yet again, the company announced tremendous results that should keep this stock moving higher as we head into 2026.

Previous coverage of the name

My most recent article on Nvidia came back in early September, right after the company reported its fiscal second quarter results. At that time, management announced revenues and earnings that beat Street estimates while giving current quarter guidance that was also nicely ahead. Given continued strong results and the potential for the growth story to continue for quite a while, I believed a $5 trillion market cap was certainly possible. Since that time, Nvidia shares have rallied more than 9%, almost triple the gain of the S&P 500 (SP500).

Looking at the Q3 results

It's incredible to think just how far Nvidia has come, perhaps the greatest growth story we've ever seen. For the October 2021 fiscal quarter just four years ago, the company reported only $4.73 billion in total revenue. As the table below shows, analyst estimates have continued to soar over time, with the average for this year's fiscal third quarter topping $55 billion, or nearly 57% growth over the prior-year period. Since late February of 2023, the cumulative revenue average estimate total for this fiscal year and the next two has risen by about $700 billion.

For the quarter, Nvidia ended up reporting revenues of more than $57 billion, handily beating the street by nearly $2 billion, showing growth of 62% over fiscal Q3 2025. Data Center revenues were $51.2 billion, growing faster than the corporate total. Gaming and Automotive grew in the low 30s, percentage-wise, while Professional Visualization saw a 56% surge and OEM/Other revenues were up 79%. As management had previously alluded to, gross margins have started to rise again thanks to the continued ramp of Blackwell chip sales. Non-GAAP gross margins, while still down 1.4 percentage points from the year-ago period, were up by 0.9 points sequentially to 73.6%.

Non-GAAP operating expenses rose by 38%, well below the level of revenues, helping adjusted operating income to match revenue growth at 62%. On the bottom line, non-GAAP earnings per share came in at $1.30, beating the street by four cents and up 60% year over year. Nvidia delivered almost $32 billion in quarterly net income. The strong results were summed up in the following press release quote from CEO Jensen Huang:

“Blackwell sales are off the charts, and cloud GPUs are sold out,” said Jensen Huang, founder and CEO of NVIDIA. “Compute demand keeps accelerating and compounding across training and inference — each growing exponentially. We’ve entered the virtuous cycle of AI. The AI ecosystem is scaling fast — with more new foundation model makers, more AI startups, across more industries, and in more countries. AI is going everywhere, doing everything, all at once.”

Perhaps even more impressive was the current quarter's guidance. Nvidia is forecasting revenues to be $65 billion, plus or minus 2%. With the street not even expecting $62 billion for the January 2026 fiscal Q4 period, those revenue estimates seen above are expected to rise even further. Non-GAAP gross margins are expected to be 75% in the quarter, another sequential rise that should put them nicely ahead of the respective year ago period.

Balance sheet and cash flow update

With the dramatic increase in revenues and profits in recent years, Nvidia has become a major free cash flow generator. Over $21 billion was generated in the third quarter alone, up more than $5 billion from the year ago period. The company does pay a small dividend, but the larger capital return was from the buyback, with over $12 billion worth of shares repurchased. The diluted share count used for Q3 EPS was actually reduced by more than 1% year over year.

Total cash marketable securities finished the period at $60.6 billion. A year earlier, that number was $38.5 billion, while the company's balance sheet held nearly $8.5 billion in total debt at the end of both periods. Nvidia is putting a lot of that cash generation towards share repurchases. While that doesn't bring the share count down tremendously when your valuation is over $4 trillion, every little bit helps. There is still $62 billion remaining on the current buyback plan, which probably will last about another year.

The current valuation picture

As of Wednesday's close, Nvidia shares went for a little more than 27 times their January 2027 expected earnings. Competitor Advanced Micro Devices, Inc. (AMD) went for nearly 35 times its calendar 2026 expectation, while Intel Corporation (INTC) is at more than 58 times, thanks to Intel's limited adjusted profitability. Nvidia is projected to show the most revenue growth among the three, despite working off a tough base number given the explosive growth seen in recent years.

Nvidia is expected to show about 50% EPS growth in its next fiscal year, the lowest of the bunch, but that's partially because Intel is coming off such a low base. AMD is projected for more than 60% EPS growth in 2026, but that's after only about 20% that's forecast for 2025. The near 50% growth for Nvidia is on top of the more than 52% currently expected for the current fiscal year, so its growth is the most impressive of the bunch.

As for Street analysts, the picture is exceptionally bullish. Of the 64 that had a rating on the stock in the past three months, 49 had strong buys and 10 had buys, with almost all the rest being holds (there was one strong sell). The average price target currently stands at roughly $235, implying about 26% upside from Wednesday's close. That average has moved up nearly $30 since my previous article on the name, jumping since the fiscal Q2 report, and I'm guessing it will head even higher after Wednesday's good news.

Final thoughts and recommendation

Nvidia announced another strong set of results on Wednesday, sending the stock higher in the after-hours session. Revenues and adjusted earnings both beat the Street by nice margins, and guidance for the current period was well ahead of expectations. Even with such a large market cap, the tremendous net income produced is leading to strong free cash flow generation, allowing a large share buyback program that's now helping to bring the share count down a little.

Given the continued solid results and reasonable valuation, I have to keep my buy rating in place. Analyst estimates will only continue to rise from here, and I could easily see the stock hitting a new high over the next year. The growth story here is extremely impressive given the revenue base, and you are paying less for it than the company's two closest competitors. The ongoing Artificial Intelligence boom is alive and well, and Nvidia is the best way to play it.

data.title: VXX: The VIX Is Rich, Sell Volatility Into Year-End (Downgrade)
data.description:
Investors are on edge heading into the holidays. That’s not a simple opinion—the Cboe Volatility Index (VIX) hovers in the notorious mid-20s zone. That’s a dubious range, as historical data reveal that forward S&P 500 returns tend to be weakest in this “no-man's land” sort of area.

What's more, implied volatility on Nasdaq 100 stocks (as measured by the VXN Index) printed multi-month highs heading into NVIDIA’s much-anticipated Q3 earnings report.

Today, I’m revisiting the iPath® Series B S&P 500® VIX Short-Term Futures™ ETN (VXX). It's a tradable proxy for volatility across the U.S. stock market, but I assert it should only be used to execute a short-term view on volatility. Due to its futures contract holdings, the fund can suffer from price decay.

I had a "Hold" rating on the ETN back in June, and while the VIX is higher compared to then, VXX has lost 28%. So, be sure to keep your time horizon short if you consider going long. But today, I’m downgrading the product to a "Sell." I see current volatility readings as overly pricey, which increases the chances of a year-end volatility drain. You can read my thoughts on the broader stock market here.

VIX: Elevated in the Mid-20s

VXN: Near A 7-Month High

According to the issuer, VXX tracks the S&P 500 VIX Short-Term Futures Index, but it’s an unsecured ETN from Barclays with no principal protection. All payments depend on the issuer’s creditworthiness, and the product carries significant risk, including potential loss of principal. The Index offers exposure to a daily rolling long position in the first and second month VIX futures contracts and reflects market participants’ views of the future direction of the VIX index at the time of expiration of the VIX futures contracts comprising the Index.

VXX is a small ETF with just $896 million in assets under management as of Nov. 17, 2025. Its annual expense ratio is high at 89 basis points, while there is no trailing 12-month dividend yield.

Share-price momentum is poor, but VXX has turned up lately, consistent with the VIX ascent. Moreover, volume has been on the rise, and I’ll note the full technical situation later in the article. As it stands, average daily volume is north of 8 million shares, and the 30-day median bid/ask spread is not too wide at six basis points.

What might get VXX going? Well, according to the November Bank of America Global Fund Manager Survey, a plurality of respondents—45%--view AI as being in potential bubble territory. That's a steep rise from the October report, and well above September’s results. It aligns with the VXN volatility outperformance compared to other indices, too.

Bigger picture, high U.S. stock market valuations pose downside risks on the surface, but history suggests that strong January through October gains often portend further equity upside—something I detailed in the Vanguard Total Stock Market ETF (VTI) write-up I linked to at the onset of this piece.

AI Bubble Talk Grows Louder

What makes me skeptical of volatility right now is the reality that historical (realized) volatility has been massively low compared to the current VIX level. The spread today is about eight figures, suggesting that there’s an undue bid to volatility, and investors are reaching for volatility protection.

Of course, we could see wider stock market swings as official data begins trickling in after the 40-day and 40-night government shutdown that ended earlier this month. What’s more, at the time of this writing, NVIDIA (NVDA) is about to report quarterly results. That stock is 8% of the SPX, and with an implied move of 7%, that naturally puts upward pressure on S&P 500 options pricing.

And we can’t ignore what’s happening at the Fed. The FOMC votes on its next interest rate decision on Dec. 10. That could be among the final volatility catalysts of the year. A hawkish hold is a clear risk that would send the VIX higher.

Still, I expect calmer heads to prevail and cooler volatility through Dec. 31.

VIX Futures Term Structure Holding in the Low-Mid 20s

Speaking of seasonality, I can’t ignore that December has been historically weak for VXX. Per Seeking Alpha’s calendar price data, the ETN has traded lower in all but two Decembers going back to 2018. November is also a bearish stretch, typically.

VXX: Bearish Year-End Trends

The Technical Take

With the VIX running high near 24, VXX’s technical situation is more bearish than bullish. Notice in the chart below that the $41 mark has been an important pivot point over the past few years. To be clear, VXX technicals are not as useful as, say, a liquid large-cap stock, but we can still glean insights. Supporting upside price action with the ETN is the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph—it has been putting in a series of higher lows since July.

But take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average. It’s on the decline, suggesting that the bears control the primary trend. The volume increase lately is curious and could spell some accumulation, but there remains a high amount of volume by price beginning in the low $40s that will make it tough on the VXX bulls. Support is in the low $30s.

VXX: $41 Resistance, Falling 200dma, $32 Support

The Bottom Line

I have a "Sell" rating on VXX. I see this volatility proxy as likely to decline into year-end, given the apparent premium being put on near-term VIX futures contracts. A bullish equity view aligns with lower implied volatility in the weeks ahead, too.

data.title: Cryptocurrencies: Bitcoin Plummets To 7-Month Low
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

This weekly update tracks some of the largest cryptocurrencies by market share: bitcoin (BTC-USD) and ether (ETH-USD). While both are considered to be high-risk when it comes to investing, the two have foundational differences that investors should know.

We've also included XRP (XRP-USD), as it was one of the largest cryptocurrencies when this series began. According to Wikipedia, a cryptocurrency is "a digital asset designed to work as a medium of exchange that uses cryptography to secure its transactions, to control the creation of additional units, and to verify the transfer of assets."

Bitcoin

Bitcoin was the world's first cryptocurrency and decentralized digital currency. The first bitcoin transaction occurred in early 2009 and has since grown worldwide to a mainstream financial asset. It is often considered volatile, as seen in our first chart, but one can argue that it is also resilient.

Bitcoin's closing price plummeted to its lowest level since April this week. BTC is now down ~2% year-to-date and sits ~25% below its record close from October 2025.

Ether

Ether is a cryptocurrency run on the Ethereum blockchain platform and was launched in July 2015. It has the second largest market share, despite being the newest of the three discussed in this article.

Ether's closing price fell more than 15% this week to its lowest level in four months. ETH is now up ~7% year-to-date and is ~35% below its record close from August 2025.

XRP

XRP, which is owned by Ripple, was launched in 2012 and was one of the larger cryptocurrencies for some time until new coins joined the market.

Bitcoin vs. Ether vs. XRP

An index has been created in order to chart these three cryptocurrencies together, considering their significantly different pricing histories. A logarithmic scale is used on the y-axis of this chart to better illustrate the relative percentage changes and long-term growth of these cryptocurrencies, as opposed to their absolute price fluctuations.

The chart tells us which cryptocurrency's price has changed the most since November 9, 2017. At one point or another, all three have been at the top. At the time of writing, bitcoin is in the lead.

On January 10th, 2024, the SEC approved spot bitcoin ETFs from a range of issuers such as Grayscale Bitcoin Trust ETF (GBTC), iShares Bitcoin Trust (IBIT), Fidelity Wise Origin Bitcoin Fund (FBTC), ARK 21Shares Bitcoin ETF (ARKB), Bitwise Bitcoin ETF (BITB), Coinshares Valkyrie Bitcoin Fund (BRRR). Here are some of the quick takeaways from the spot bitcoin ETF launch.

On July 23rd, 2024, a handful of spot ether ETFs launched from a range of issuers such as Grayscale Ethereum Trust (ETHE), Franklin Ethereum ETF (EZET), Bitwise Ethereum ETF (ETHW), iShares Ethereum Trust (ETHA), Fidelity Ethereum ETF (FETH).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Did Berkshire Just Endorse AI? 13F Review
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Berkshire Hathaway Inc. (BRK.B) just released its 13F filing, and the most significant move has been the significant addition of Alphabet (GOOGL), which is now one of the fund’s top 10 holdings.

Many analysts and investors have jumped to call this an AI endorsement, but we should be wary about reading too much into this move.

Alphabet is, as I have defended multiple times, the best Mag 7 to own, and this is regardless of the AI narrative.

In fact, in many ways buying Alphabet is the most conservative way of gaining exposure to AI and even other themes like Space Exploration, Autonomous driving and even Quantum computing.

All in all, I see this as a good move for Berkshire, but the fund remains defensive, with a record amount of cash on balance.

Following record Q3 results, I maintain my Buy rating on Berkshire, as it continues to execute in its core business and provides investors with investment optionality.

Berkshire 13F Movements

Let’s begin by quickly going through the latest moves as per Berkshire’s 13F.

Key changes:

New position: Alphabet (Google)

The big headline was the addition of just shy of 18 million shares in Alphabet, amounting to a $4.3 billion investment.

Alphabet now represents over 1.5% of Berkshire’s portfolio, making it a top 10 holding.

Berkshire has historically been very selective with “pure” tech: Apple (AAPL) (which Buffett frames as a consumer brand), Amazon, (AMZN) and now Alphabet.

It notably passed on Google in the early 2000s despite using it to power Geico’s customer acquisition, something Buffett previously labelled as a “mistake.”

Adds and top-ups

The 13F also shows a cluster of smaller additions/

Chubb (CB): Stake increased by ~16%, a meaningful addition to what is becoming a core insurance position.

Domino’s Pizza (DPZ): Boosted by ~13%, signaling confidence in this asset-light, high-ROIC consumer compounder.

Lennar (LEN) and Sirius XM (SIRI): Smaller adds, likely the work of the lieutenants rather than Buffett himself.

Trims and exits

On the sell side, Berkshire continued a pattern that has been in place for several quarters: harvesting gains, re-weighting risk, and letting cash build.

Apple (AAPL): Another ~15% cut, down to about 238m shares, still Berkshire’s largest holding at around $60 billion, but much smaller than the peak.

Bank of America (BAC): Trimmed by roughly 6%.

Verisign (VRSN): Reduced by roughly one-third.

DaVita (DVA) and Nucor (NUE): Smaller reductions.

D.R. Horton (DHI): Position fully exited.

In total, Berkshire bought about $6.4 billion of stock and sold roughly $12.5 billion, with the difference helping push cash to over $381 billion.

This is not the behavior of a manager leaning hard into a new bull market. It’s the behavior of someone who still thinks most of the market is expensive, but with one notable exception.

Why Google?

There are three big reasons that, in my view, explain the addition of Alphabet to Berkshire’s portfolio.

Economics: A tollbooth on global attention

Alphabet today is effectively three giant businesses under one umbrella: Search & Network ads, YouTube and Google Cloud

Together they throw off enormous cash.

On most estimates, Alphabet is running at tens of billions of dollars in annual free cash flow with structurally high margins and relatively modest capital intensity.

YouTube and Cloud are picking up steam, while Search is defending its market much better than was expected last year.

That translates directly into the sort of long-duration, predictable earnings stream Berkshire likes to own.

Moat: habit, data and infrastructure

Alphabet’s core moat still looks very “Buffett-like.”

Search, Chrome and Android are embedded as defaults in billions of user journeys. Changing that behavior at scale would require huge incentives and coordination.

Every query, map route and YouTube view feeds back into better products and more effective ads. That’s a classic scale advantage thanks to data and model feedback loops.

Lastly, Google has spent years and hundreds of billions on data centers, networks and in-house silicon, which is not something a start-up can easily replicate.

AI models like Gemini sit on top of all that. They enhance the moat, but they’re not the moat itself.

Valuation: the “cheapest” of the mega-caps

For much of 2025, Alphabet traded at a forward P/E under 20x despite being one of the most profitable and diversified businesses in big tech.

Even after the recent pop, it’s around the mid-20s on forward earnings.

That isn’t “deep value,” but relative to structurally higher-multiple peers in cloud and AI, and given the quality, growth and balance sheet strength you’re getting, it’s easy to see how Berkshire could frame this as a value opportunity rather than a momentum trade.

Put that together, and Alphabet starts to resemble Apple in Buffett’s framing.

A consumer-utility platform with a tech wrapper, not a fragile growth story.

So is this an AI endorsement?

Not in the way the market wants it to be.

Alphabet is absolutely an AI leader. Gemini 3.0, AI-enhanced search, and agent-like tools are major internal growth drivers and a big part of the bull case.

But look at Berkshire’s moves as a whole

  • Continuing to cut Apple, not add to it.

  • Trimming banks.

  • Sitting on record cash.

  • Staying a net seller of equities for three years running.

That’s not the portfolio of someone suddenly convinced we’re at the start of a once-in-a-generation AI melt-up. It’s the portfolio of someone who is being cautious, but has clearly identified Google as a winner no matter what.

To me, Berkshire bought Google because it is the best big tech company they can understand and justify on cash flows. And because they needed to plant a flag in the AI theme.

My 2 Cents

Berkshire posted strong Q3 results, with operating profit increasing 34% YoY.

The core businesses continue to do well, and with the large amount of cash in the books, I still think Berkshire can be a good addition to a well-diversified portfolio.

The stock has bounced nicely after the post-Buffett retirement dip and is now holding steady above the key weekly EMAs, with momentum to the upside possible

Final Thoughts

Berkshire’s latest 13F will be remembered as the day they "caved" and bought Google.

But if you zoom out, nothing about the underlying philosophy has changed. This is still the same playbook.

Concentrate in a handful of dominant, cash-rich franchises, avoid overpaying, even for great stories and also keep a ludicrous amount of optionality in cash for when markets finally crack.

Alphabet fits that playbook almost perfectly. It just happens to sit at the crossroads of AI, cloud and digital advertising.

So yes, Berkshire bought Google. But it did so because, in its eyes, and mine, Google is the best large-cap tech business money can buy today.

If anything, the lesson is the opposite: when everyone else is chasing the theme, quietly buy the cash-gushing platform that will still be here after the AI hype cycle has moved on.
